subject: Set master as branch in .gitmodules
commit_message:
Set master as branch in .gitmodules

It avoids the next fatal error when updating gitmodules from doc
(double levels of submodules).

Submodule (kubernetes/aai) branch configured to inherit branch from
superproject, but the superproject is not on any branch.

Issue-ID: DOC-505

Signed-off-by: Cédric Ollivier <cedric.ollivier@orange.com>
Change-Id: Id2d6434210003c20de74fc6da87125c54506cfd1

Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

Could you please provide the exact command that fails and error message?

Actually I'm not very happy with this solution as it requires to remember to change the branch every time we create a new one...

Giving -1 just to help you notice this comment

Patch Set 1:

Could you please try to sync submodules in doc dir? It will highlight the issue.
  $ git submodule update --init --recursive --remote

As far as I understand, Doc must be fully refactored if this patch is not merged.
It's worth mentioning that they are 2 levels of submodules here.

Patch Set 1:

Now I see your point but for me it rather looks like some bug in git. I'm just trying to debug it now and see if it could be easily fixed in upstream

Patch Set 1:

Ok, I investigated this issue a little bit more and here is the output:

1) git submodule update --init --recursive --remote

This command behaves correctly. It actually should fail in this case because after you init the submodule a checkout using commit hash is performed. So technically the repo is in a detached head state. Additionally according to the doc the default update strategy is checkout so it would also leave the repo in detached head.

2) git submodule init

This is the source of all the issues. The problem is that this command always clones the repo and leaves it in detached head state which causes for example the --rebase strategy of update command to not work out of the box as described in the doc. I consider this to be a bug or at least lack of some functionality so I'll report this to the git community

3) Temporary solution

It is actually possible to workaround all those issues if you prepare your repo correctly and then use --rebase strategy for submodule update. I made a small script which clones the doc repo and sets up everything as required:

 #!/bin/bash
 
 function prepare_submodules {
 	git submodule update --init
 	local SUBMODULE_LIST=`git submodule--helper status | awk '{print $2}'`
 	while read -r submod; do
 		local PARENT_DIR=`pwd`
 		local BRANCH=`git submodule--helper remote-branch ${submod}`
 
 		cd ${submod}
 		git branch -f ${BRANCH} HEAD;
 		git checkout ${BRANCH}
 		if [ ! -z "`git submodule status`" ]; then
 			prepare_submodules;
 		fi
 		cd ${PARENT_DIR}
 	done <<< "$SUBMODULE_LIST"
 }
 
 git clone ssh://kopasiak@gerrit.onap.org:29418/doc
 cd doc
 prepare_submodules

After you clone the repo with this script you get all submodules recursively initialized and all checked out to the local branch with a suitable name so after that you can use simply:

 git submodule update --remote --rebase --recursive

and there is no need to change anything inside the repo itself thus I'd prefer to leave this -1 as is.

Patch Set 1:

I strongly think that the current doc design forces to explicitly write the branch.
Even if git is enhanced upstream, how could you manage patchset verification in doc as the ref is local to doc? It should be the same for oom patchset verification, no?

Patch Set 1:

-1 for the temporarily solution.
